So the person you're about to meet, let's go to John Wood. My God. Come on. Oh, God, this is good. God, it's so good. 99 years, 98 years old, I mean 99 in eight weeks. I was just in my car praying that it was John Wood. Good to hear. Oh, my God. So now you know why I gave you the book. Oh, my God. God, it's good. I'm going to say, you're good. So good to see you again, coach. You're going to say you're good. This is Joaquin Hawkins. Nice to see you again. God bless you. Thank you. God bless you. He has, but I want him to continue. He will. I have a little while to go, yeah. He will. You talk to him from down here and from down now and then. He definitely will bless you and bless all of this. Thank you. Yeah. So we're going to come sit over here. Coach Wooden, can I take you over there? Is that okay? Sure, man. Where are you? Come on in. Come on in. All right, I'm going to sit over here. I'm going to sit over here. Coach Wooden, can I take you over there? Is that okay? Sure, man. Where ever I'm supposed to go. I'm going to help. I'm going to help. God, this is really job-ridden. Yeah. Coach, do you need any water or anything? I want to make sure you're taken care of. I'm just fine. Okay. Thank you. Thank you. So I really, really appreciate the privilege to be able to be with you again after 19 years. We last, we're sitting down together when I was 30 and he was 80. Wow. And he'll be 99 in a couple of weeks, just a few weeks away. And the reason I brought you together to sit down with us is, Boutou, assist my friend Joaquin here. He's been through some challenges I shared with you. But also I think this country, so many people have seen their dreams dashed and they feel like they don't have much of a future. And you were born in 1910. So, you know, you were in your 20s, you went through the Depression with Nellie. And your 30s, you know, you went through the experience of World War II. And you created a life that's just extraordinary. Everyone knows the legendary numbers. 88 victories in a row, four straight seasons, four perfect seasons, 10 out of 12 NCAA championships. But I know that you're most proud of the way you shape men's lives or help them to shape their lives. And so, and I know there's the winningest coach in history. You told me when we met 19 years ago that you almost never spoke about winning. That's true. So I thought maybe we could start with that and say, how do you win and not speak about winning? Well, most people take winning as the score. I don't think winning is necessarily the score. It could be, and very well might be, but not necessarily so. I think winning to me comes within. I know whether I made the effort to do the best of which I'm capable. You don't know that. You don't know that. I'm the only person that knows that. And if I can say that with honesty and feeling that I made the effort to do the best of which I'm capable, if I can say that I'm lost. I don't care what the score is. So, you know, you have to really believe that though. You can't just, you have to really believe it. And if you don't believe it, it's not affecting. There's a lot of things like that way that you have to believe. You have to believe that you get stronger through adversity. You're going to have, we're all going to have, say, life itself is peaks and valleys. And the main thing is how we react to both. We must not let either one take over the peak of the valley. And once we do, we're hurting. But life can be great. Life can be great, but we make it. Yeah, when you do those adversity times, I mean, I know you were saying that life is what you make it. But through your adverse times, I mean, was it ever a point where you just seemed like everything you did just didn't feel like it was going to work? There's a time, I think, in all of our lives when we're going to have things like that. Now, what kind of a person are we? The ones that we give up? The ones that try to fight through and make the most out of it? We have to make the most out of whatever the situation is instead of crying and giving up. Coach, you have a phrase where you say that things work out best for those who, maybe you can share that with them. But I was trying to take up a moment ago. That's another one of Lincoln's things work out best for those who make the best of the way things work out. Instead of crying away anyway. I feel about our country today and we're having a depression. I'm going to overcome it. I'm confident about how soon. I don't know, but I am confident that we'll overcome it. Why do you think we have those seasons of life? Why do you think we have those ups and downs? Is there a purpose behind those winters of life? As you say, every 80 to 100 years we go through a cycle like a depression. Well, I think there's a definite reason for having those. We should all have them. If everything is good for us, it spoils us. If everything is bad for us, it spoils us too. We've got to be able to take it and make the most of it. For the best we can. One of your philosophies that I think has made you so successful has been what you learned from your father, which was not to allow anything that you couldn't control to govern your level of happiness. Can you share a little bit about how that philosophy has helped you for almost 99 years to create champions and more importantly to create men and women who are proud of themselves, who are healthy, who are fulfilled, a peace of mind? Extremely important. We have that peace of mind. And we cannot have it unless we believe that things are going to turn out as they should. Not as the way we want them to necessarily, but as they should. Accept it and go on from there. That's my feeling about it. That's coming from the greatest. That's so true. Coach Euphobie said that success, you know, this country has had so much success. And very often success makes us satisfied and satisfaction leads to challenges or failure. Most people today determine success as the accumulation of material possessions or the attainment of power, efficient of power or prestige. Or the accomplishments. But to me, they do not necessarily indicate success. They very well may, but not necessarily. To me, success is peace within my heart knowing that I've made the effort to do the best of which I'm capable. And I'm the only person that knows that. I can fool everybody else, but I can't fool myself. When I interviewed you 19 years ago, I asked you, I knew many people that asked you before, which was the greatest team you ever coached. And I'm sure you've been asked who was the best player. You usually will never say the best player for the reasons of your philosophy around team. But you told me a team that I'd never even heard of in my life. And the reason you told me was because that team maximized their potential. How important is it to have the highest score versus maximizing potential? How has that philosophy caused you to create such incredible, successful men who left you and some of the greatest championship teams in history? We must know that they had this ability to begin with. I hope I helped them bring it out. But you're not going to have that kind of a success unless you have the talent there. You don't want to have that. You're going to be just as successful whether the talent is there or not. As long as you make the effort and get those under your supervision to make the effort, to do the best of which they're capable. Then you can smile. The most amazing thing was the coach that shared with me was that the team that he mentioned was not the Luhau Sunder team. It was none of the teams that most people would know. He said the difference was many teams were talented and they had more points on the score. At the end of the day on the scoreboard. But he said they didn't really win because they didn't give their all. How do you know if you're truly successful? It's not by the scoreboard, it's by what? The feeling within your own heart, the peace of mind that you might have. I don't think you are successful unless you have peace of mind and you're not going to have peace of mind all the time. You're going to have to work to attain and maintain peace of mind. Not easy. I know you taught your players that the only way to know if they won was not by the score on the board. But whether or not they'd given a million percent or a hundred percent that moment on the court. Is that right? That's almost right Tony. But not quite. Okay, tell me. I say we can never have a hundred percent. Yes. But we should be trying to execute whatever it may be. A hundred percent. We know we're not going to get perfection, but we can try for it. I have a question. Do you think there is a such thing as being perfect? In my mind, there's no thing as perfection that can be attained. It should always be trying, always be trying for perfection. But I believe it's impossible to be perfect. You came pretty darn close to four perfect seasons. We're ten out of twelve national championships. Seven in a row and no one's even won. You know, two teams won two in a row. How do you approach getting towards perfect? What are the secrets to being the best you can possibly be? You must have dunked in your own mind that this is what I'm going to do. You must accept the fact that you're not going to make it a hundred percent, but you're going to work for it. And a little block here and there, that's not going to stop me. I'm going to come right back. I said, yes, I can. And eventually you will know whether you have... I didn't give it your best. Anyone who gives it their best. What more is there? Coach, this youngster is 36, just turned 36. So there's 63 more years ahead of you. That's two more lifetimes. And he's had the feeling Joaquin has at times that if you didn't play basketball, he wasn't a professional basketball player, then his life didn't matter. What do you say to that, Coach? It's an outside activity. Something that's good, enjoyable. But it's not your life. Not basketball, anything else. It can give you some joy. And it can give your friends and lovers that can give them joy. But there's other things that will give them more lasting joy than just any athletic thing, whatever it might be. It's great. I think sports are great. But I think there's... In many cases too much emphasis placed on it. You know, this... this mess last week you may have read where I was selected as the greatest coach of all time. Ridiculous. Ridiculous. Why is that ridiculous? You're so humble. You're trying to pick for me the greatest basketball player you've ever seen. Number one. Baseball. Football. You can't do it. And then to say the best of them all. It's ridiculous. Flattering. Nice. Yeah, but it's ridiculous and it's not... It's not good to you. You've managed to keep a sense of humility no matter how successful you've been. How have you done that? Most people, success goes to their head. Well now you're judging success a little different. I... What is your... How would you define it for yourself today? I know you came up with the definition in 1934. I've made the effort and I can honestly say I made the effort. 100%? No. But by and large I made the effort to execute near my ability level and to keep improving. But have you done it 100%? I don't think so. I'd like to say I have, but I don't think so. But I have tried, I can say that. But I like the things... My father is proud of me. And I can thank my father as he said, everyone never complained they make excuses. He said, and I think my overall philosophy came from one of the points in the seventh creed, where make each day your masterpiece. Just do the best you can each day. I can... Don't try to beat somebody else. Don't try to beat someone. Learn from others. And do the best you can. Don't try to beat him. Just learn from him. What does it mean to be a coach, this young man? Also the reason I have so much caring and heart for him and wanted to help him, coach, is he spent years coaching about 7,000 kids from inner city. He's going back and giving back. He comes from a tough environment. He's actually great with these kids. He thinks playing basketball is the ultimate. And yet, you know, we see him out there with these kids and he likes them up. So how important is coaching? What does it mean to be a coach in your mind? Well, to see how youngsters have responded to your efforts to make them understand what not basketball is, but life is, that's the important thing. Coach, what's in your 90, almost 99 years, what has been the biggest adversity you've faced and what pulled you through? I would really have to say probably that the greatest adversity that I had was when I lost my wife. How many of us? My children, friends, they helped me. And there were some other things, of course, but I think more than anything else. And I'm still fortunate having children and I had a great granddaughter. I was married just on July the 5th. And she was born about three months after I lost my wife. She came into my life as I lost somebody in my life. But getting over it, I won't say I'll ever get over it, but it helped tremendously. You told me back then that part of what happened, and she had only passed about six years before when I met you. It's obviously been 19 years now, or a little more than I guess 24 years now. You told me at that time that you can no longer as a result fear death. That's still true? Yes, and I have no fear of death. Now he's passing, how did she end up? I'll be a winner again. I don't know. I wrote something I can remember. I write things and I like to have them on the podium. But sometimes I can't remember what I wrote. But I wrote one on death. I can't think of a start. The years have left their imprint on my hands and on my face. He wrecked no longer as my walk, as slower as my pace. But there is no fear within my heart because I'm growing old. I only wish I had more time to better serve my Lord. When I'm going to him in prayer, he's brought me inner peace. And soon my cares and troubles and other worries cease. He's helped me in so many ways. He's never let me down. Why should I hear the future when soon I'll be near his ground? I know down here my time is short. There's endless time up there and he will forgive and keep me ever in his loving prayer. I know you've talked about how you didn't like all your players, but you loved them all. And that part of that was the secret to coaching. Will you mention that to us? How important is love? The four letter word is love in this life. Well, to me love is the most important word in our language. In so many ways. And love is giving. It is giving. Was it? It's kind of a bell isn't a bell until it's ringing. The song isn't a song until it's singing. And the love that is in us wasn't put there to say, love isn't love until you give it away. That's true. The whole philosophy of life has always been, would it be going on the basketball court or being with your wife or your family? It's what we give that marks our life. What we fail to give you say we lose forever. Is that true? Absolutely. That's the piece of the lesson. I understand that you sleep each night beside where your wife normally sleeps and you write her a letter every month on the 21st. Is that true? I lost her on the 21st and so I write her. I'm the 21st of each month and on the side where she slept, I have her down stretched out and everything and that's not moved until the weekly wash and all that. And then put her back again. All your letters you've accumulated, do they sit there also? Not on the bed. I did have them on the bed for quite a while but some of the great grandchildren are not there. And I had to sort of seclude them a little bit. You're love for people and you're love for your family and you're love for giving has made you such a magnificent role model to everyone. Do you have any questions for this man who, when you think that you've got a tough life or because you've got financial problems or challenges you have these three children that love you, you have your wife that loves you completely, what do you have to say to coach or ask the coach? Well, think it just with me and I commend you and how do all any type of adversity have. Like you said, love has been the most important thing for you and I too. I have that same ambition. It's about love. I love my family, I love my wife, I love my daughters and it's just still those times where I'm by myself and I just think about all my adverse times and I just don't feel like, I just don't feel like this, at times I just don't feel like it's going to get better. And I'm so not used to having that feeling because I'm a fighter. A fighter in the past I have not been able to complain about my situation or anything but since my stroke it just seemed like everything is even more harder for me and I try to contact myself with people that were good people, good people and there are still times where I have certain people take advantage of me and it's just hard for me even at times to trust myself. So my question to you, have you ever, my question to you is have you ever felt like you can't trust yourself? I would say yes. There's other things that might be worse. When I graduated from college and signed for a job, I was going to get married. I had been saving the money that I could because there were no scholarships or anything of my day and I had to take my own way through college and then after my senior year, that little time in there I placed some exhibition basketball and saved some money. I saved up $909 and a nickel. I had that in the bank. The day before Nellie and I were going to be married, the bank closed. I didn't have a nickel. But a man called me and said, Johnny, come in the seating. An envelope was pushed over and he said, baby back when you can. In this envelope it was $200. How about that? What pulled you through that? Was it just hard work and faith? I think so. So different things we must accept. We just must accept. We may not want to, but we must accept. The last two questions, Coach, if you have one more you want to ask, you let me know. Coach, very often I know that what Keen went through a very tough time, people do. He has recovered physically, but he's not completely allowed himself to own the fact that it was a miracle. More like it was a problem than a miracle. And I know that he's in his frustrations. He's blamed others, whether it be his coach or other people there, for the fact that he didn't get help sooner. What advice would you give him or anyone who's feeling a sense of anger or rage or blame towards someone for something that happened to them? We must not be excused. There are going to be reasons. And I suspect from what I've heard that you sometimes blames your coach. And you know what? You might be right sometimes. That is true. You know I had a player that played for me. He didn't get to play very much. He didn't like me. And for 25 to 30 years after I retired, he wouldn't speak to me. He would avoid me. And now he's one of my very nearest and closest friends. What changed that coach? He says that he began to think when it was making him successful. And it was the things that I was trying to teach which he didn't accept to that degree that he should. And finally he was on the golf course. And he left the golf course, called me. And wanted to come and talk to me. And the book, Be Quick But Don't Hurry, emanated it from that. Wow. Coach, one last question. I know that when I met you 19 years ago, you just had your 80th birthday. And you told me there was something you valued more than any championship you ever had. And it was an audio you were given on your 80th birthday. I don't know if you recall. But it was a song that was sung to you. And do you remember that? You played that for me. And young man, would you share the story with him about what is your deepest, most top moment of your career here? The thing you're most proud of was not a championship. What was it? Well, certainly not having one of my players sing his eyes on the sparrow. One of my favorite champions. And of course there are other things too. But he was a player that didn't really start, right? He was a player that didn't play very much. He was in the bench most of the time. And yet Coach touched his life so deeply that 25 years later he was still saying how he touched his life, even though he didn't play basketball most of the time. He's very dear. He comes regularly. I said I'm a loving person. And that was very, and it's been hard for me to accept that even though that might have been true, but for me to forgive my coach, to forgive my team, and I want to, in my heart I really want to forgive them. And I'm sure I will. But it's a lot easier for me now talking with you, seeing that you have the same type of adversities. And that it's basically an excuse. And you can't live your life. You can't live your life having excuses. Have you read much about Mother Teresa? She said forgiveness sets you free. When you stop and think about that, as long as you have something bothering in your heart, you're going to suffer. When you can forgive and let it go, you're free. That's a beautiful coach. Coach, you are one of the wisest and most beautiful human beings that I've had privileged to meet in my 49 years. I can't thank you enough for taking the time for Joaquin today and for me to have the privilege to be with you again as well. And I wish for you every beautiful moment. I do have to ask you one more question though is, for relationships, you were together for 53 years with Nellie. You know, almost 53, right? I remember correctly. What advice would you give couples to have a magnificent relationship, a masterpiece of a relationship today? So many people are selfish today. What would you say to them? Love. Love. It's that one word. Love will take care of everything. You're going to have disagreements. Love will take care of everything. This is the truth of love. It's not all problems of that nature. It's not all financial problems. It's not all things that are more important than financial. Most people don't think there are any more important things than everything depends on money or position of power. So the best you've learned in 99 years is that it all comes back to love. Is that right? That's right. God bless you coach. Thank you so much for your time. You're so wonderful to be with you again. Thank you coach. Thank you so much for your time. I know you had a busy schedule and I just really thank you. My pleasure. What do you promise him? Not just because he didn't talk to you. There's got to be something given back. What are you going to show up and deliver? Effort. Even though I want to be perfect, perfection is not something that it's not... You can try for it. You can try for it but it's not a necessity. Absolutely. It's not a necessity. I love it. I mean loving my friends and my wife. I'm going to continue to give it back because that's something that I love to do with the kids. No excuses. There's no need to have any excuses in life. Love. Not just your wife and your friends. That's your enemies. That's very important. Wow. This is amazing. Oh my gosh. Thank you coach. Who you are penetrates more than anything anybody could ever say. Thank you. You walk your talk you have for 99 years and your father must be very proud of you. You lived by that code that he gave you so many years ago. Do you have one of those codes we can give Joaquin? The code his father gave him when he was young man. He lived by his entire life. Definition of success. When I was 14. I was 14. Graduated from a grade school. 7. Is this 7 point Creed? 7 point Creed. All he said when he gave that to me was trying to follow this. Never lie. Never cheat. Never steal. Don't lie. Don't complain. And don't make excuses. And live this way. Be true to yourself. Help others. Make each day your masterpiece. Drink deeply from good books including the good books. Make friendship a fine heart. Build a shelter against a rainy day. Pray for guidance. Childhood give thanks for your blessings each day. Thank you coach. You got it. Lock it. There's no greater joy or pleasure than to do something that you feel definitely helped another. And if I could have helped Joaquin in any way to face some problems. And he's had problems. We all have problems. You have to face them and learn to know that life itself is peaks and valleys. And you have to learn each one is to blame others in any way. This is what I live for. I live for those moments. And you could see the breakthroughs happening in Joaquin's mind and more importantly in his heart. When he's listening to this man who he respects so much. Look at him straight in the eye and say blaming others is the only failure. It's just an excuse you're using. And letting him know that the relationship he has with his wife is the most important thing in the world with his children. And that he doesn't need to forgive this will set you free. And Joaquin saying yeah I love and I forgive my family and my friends. He said no no no forgive your enemies. And that moment when Joaquin's eyes opened up and a tear started to come down. He's a six foot seven man. I can cry with the best of him too. But it was good to see his heart pierced. Oh my gosh. He has permitted adversity to affect him in the wrong way. He is not accepting that and trying to overcome it and concentrate on the things that are really important. I have a feeling that maybe a little conversation we've had maybe help out time and I pray it will be. For Joaquin to see and hear those peaks and valleys from somebody who made it through the depression with his wife in his twenties and his brother in his thirties had to go through a world war and has been through every up and down you can imagine and still has this incredible graciousness, this incredible appreciation for life and for people. And who can tell him that all the money is going to go away, all the NBA basketball is not your life. Love. Love is your life. I won't say this guy is limit and for him no. I won't say that going too far but I think he's going to do alright. That's the thing you call it you see. You want this thing you can stop. So now what's wonderful is the good soul matches the behavior. You know since he's lived in a village and been in a position where he's gotten his butt kicked and he's had to deal with adversity and have a real work ethic and he's stormed through fire and he's made himself physically stronger than he thought he was. And today is I think one of the final steps, not the final step, but one of the final steps in his transformation into owning himself again as a man and as a father and stepping up and being responsible and letting go of the stories and excuses as real as they may be. I've got some tasks ahead that are going to get him to realize that the journey never ends and there's got to be a new level of standard in him that he doesn't just talk about and feel but shows up. We all need to remember that it's simple and trite as it sounds that adversity is our strength. It is a gift. It's the only reason we grow. It's the only way we get better and we're going through very tough times in this country right now and my hope is a show like this will remind people that we have no problems. We've got some significant opportunities to grow and Coach Wooden has proven that for 99 years and hopefully we can emulate just a little bit of that. If we do, we're going to have a richer life with a lot more joy and a lot more love. The next step in the journey begins now.
